coredata.dc:title: Photosynthetic-bacteria-derived red emissive carbon dots with low toxicity for lysosomal imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jingmin
An, Jing
Zhang, Guanghui
Wang, Ruoyu
Xu, Yifan
Zhu, Fenfang
Shen, Kangliang
Wang, Yongzhong
coredata.dc:description: A novel red emissive carbon dots (CDs) derived from photosynthetic bacteria are synthesized successfully via a simple microwave–assisted hydrothermal process. The CDs exhibit good water solubility, biocompatibility, and red photoluminescence emission with an absolute quantum yield of 15.17% in water. More importantly, 5 mg/ml CDs present low toxicity after co–cultured with human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) and HeLa cells. Furthermore, the red emissive CDs can clearly image the HUVECs and HeLa cells within 10 min and maintain stable emission for 1 h. It is also proved that the organelle of CD imaging is mainly lysosome. This work provides a new method to prepare long–wavelength fluorescence–emission CDs and explores a new material with potential applications in the biomedical field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomaterials
Carbon materials
Photosynthetic-bacteria
Green synthesis
Red emissive carbon dots
Lysosomal imaging

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was financed by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Y Z Wang, grant No. 31770066 and 31470218) and the Open Fund for Anhui Key Laboratory of Modern Biomanufacturing of Anhui University (J M Wang.). All co–authors acknowledge the support of the Key Laboratory of Human Microenvironment and Precision Medicine of Anhui Higher Education Institutes, Anhui University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthesis and cell imaging application of red emissive CDs derived from PSB. (b) Typical TEM image (inset: HRTEM image) and (c) particle size distribution of the reductant CDs.
(a) UV–vis and PL emission spectra (λex = 400 nm) of red emissive CDs in water. (b) MTT results of HeLa and HUVECs treated with different concentrations of the red emissive CDs for 12 h.
CLSM images of (a) HeLa cells and (b) HUVECs co–incubated with 1 mg/mL red emissive CDs for different times. Scale bar: 50 μm.

head.ce:title: Photosynthetic-bacteria-derived red emissive carbon dots with low toxicity for lysosomal imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, Anhui, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Sciences, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, Anhui, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hefei No.8 High School, Hefei 230601, Anhui, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hefei No.8 High School, Hefei 230601, Anhui, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel red emissive carbon dots (CDs) derived from photosynthetic bacteria are synthesized successfully via a simple microwave–assisted hydrothermal process. The CDs exhibit good water solubility, biocompatibility, and red photoluminescence emission with an absolute quantum yield of 15.17% in water. More importantly, 5 mg/ml CDs present low toxicity after co–cultured with human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) and HeLa cells. Furthermore, the red emissive CDs can clearly image the HUVECs and HeLa cells within 10 min and maintain stable emission for 1 h. It is also proved that the organelle of CD imaging is mainly lysosome. This work provides a new method to prepare long–wavelength fluorescence–emission CDs and explores a new material with potential applications in the biomedical field.

ce:section.ce:para: Widespread attention has been paid to carbon dots (CDs) in the recent years owing to their several advantages such as simple manufacturing, unique optical properties, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility [1,2]. Such nanomaterials have potential applications in many fields including bioimaging [3], nanomedicine [4], catalysis [5] and so forth [6,7]. As one of the earliest applications of CDs, the exploration of bioimaging application of CDs has attracted much attention [3]. However, most of the known CDs exhibit blue to green photoluminescence (PL) emission with short penetration to organisms and may cause damage to tissues and cells, which restricts their further use in biomedical applications [8].

Although the research on long wavelength emissive CDs has yielded fruitful results, most of the materials used in the synthesis are organic reagents, requiring sophisticated chemical synthesis or complex purification steps. [2,4,9]. Meanwhile, an increasing number of studies have examined the synthesis and applications of CDs derived from bacteria, which are one of the most abundant species in all organisms [10,11]. In particular, Ge et al. [12] prepared novel red PL emissive CDs with Hypocrella bambusae (HB) as the raw carbon source by a solvothermal method, which could be utilized as bimodal fluorescence/photoacoustic imaging agents to guide cancer treatment. It is proved that using bacteria as carbon source can simply and quickly prepare red emissive CDs with good application prospects.

Photosynthetic bacteria (PSB), are a type of photosynthetic prokaryote widely distributed in nature [13]. It is believed that PSB are potential biomaterials for the preparation of CDs with long wavelength emissions. Herein, a new CDs derived from PSB with red PL emission without any further surface treatment or silica–column chromatography were obtained. Moreover, the resultant CDs exhibit good water solubility, biocompatibility, low toxicity and good cytolysosomal imaging effect, which make them desirable for bioimaging applications.

Red emissive CDs were synthesized via a simple microwave–assisted hydrothermal treatment of PSB (Fig. 1 a). Briefly, 2 g of PSB was mixed thoroughly in 20 mL of distilled water. This mixture was then heated for 10 min using a domestic microwave oven (800 W, Galanz, China) until the solution turned brown. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the brown solution was filtered through a 0.22 µm filter membrane several times and then dialyzed (MWCO of 500) for 72 h in ultrapure water to remove the residual reactants. Finally, the dialyzed solution was freeze–dried at −60 °C under vacuum to obtain a milky white powder.

As shown in Fig. 1b, the obtained CDs were quasi–spherical and uniform in size with an average diameter of 3.9 nm (Fig. 1c). There are some aggregates in CDs, which may be caused by the cross–linking of groups on the surface. The inset in Fig. 1b displays clear lattice fringes, and the interplanar spacing of 0.242 nm is close to the (110) diffraction facet of graphite carbon [14].

To further illustrate the structure of CDs, XRD pattern, XPS and FT-IR spectra were conducted. The XRD pattern (Fig. S1a) present two peaks at 22.1° and 75.3°, which can be attributed to the (001) and (110) planes of carbon, respectively [11,15,16]. The surveyed XPS spectrum (Fig. S1b) indicates that the reductant CDs mainly consist of C (68.42 at.%), O (24.97 at.%), N (5.06 at.%) and S (0.71 at.%). The C 1 s spectra (Fig. S1c) revealed three typical peaks that could be attributed to C–C/C = C (284.8 eV), C–O/C–N (286.2), and C = O (288.4 eV), respectively [16,17]. Moreover, the N 1 s spectra (Fig. S1d) could be deconvoluted into three peaks at 399.6, 400.1, and 400.6 eV, which could be assigned to Pyridinic-N, Pyrrolic-N, and Graphitic-N, respectively [15]. Further, in the FT-IR spectrum shown in Fig. S2, the peaks at 3289 and 1657 cm−1 could be ascribed to the presence of the –OH and –CONH species [18], which is consistent with the results of the XPS analysis, proving that the surface of the red emissive CDs is rich in nitrogen- and oxygen-containing functional groups.

As shown in Fig. 2 a, the as–synthesized CDs shows wide UV–vis absorption from 200 to 700 nm, where the peak at 230 nm and 280 nm can be assigned to the π → π* transition of the sp2 domain and the n → π* transition, respectively [11,19]. And the peak at 405 nm may be caused by the strong electron interaction between the groups. Significantly, CDs aqueous solution exhibits red PL emission under a 365 nm UV lamp (the inset of Fig. 2a). As shown in Fig. S3a, the PL emission of CDs is excitation-dependent with the excitation light shift from 340 nm to 440 nm. Three PL emission centered at 480, 595, and 642 nm can be observed obviously under the excitation of 400 nm, giving the CDs a long–wavelength PL emission in the region from green to red. In addition, the absolute quantum yield (QY) of the CDs under 340 nm excitation is 15.17%, while under 400 nm excitation is 8.8%, which is comparable to that of the other red emissive CDs [8]. These results indicate that red emissive CDs with good water solubility and high QY can be synthesized successfully.

The cytotoxicity of red emissive CDs was explored in Fig. 2b, the survival rates of HeLa or HUVECs were approximately 90% after co–culturing with different concentrations (0.001–5 mg/ml) of CDs for 12 h, indicating that the CDs have low cytotoxicity. In addition, the survival rates of HUVECs was still ∼90% when the concentration of the CDs was increased to 10 mg/ml. By contrast, the survival rate of HeLa cells was only 20%, suggesting that that high concentrations of the CDs may selectively kill cancer cells. The results prove that the CDs possess low toxicity, which is important for use in biological applications.

Fig. 3 presents the CLSM images of HeLa cells and HUVECs co–incubated with the CDs (1 mg/ml) for different times. The red emissive CDs can light up cells in 10 min and effectively maintain for 60 min. Furthermore, Fig. S4 shows that the CDs are almost entirely distributed in the cytoplasm that does not affect DNA replication and transcription, further reducing the toxicity of the CDs, which is consistent with the results in Fig. 2b. In addition, as shown Fig. S5, lysosome colocalization demonstrates that the CDs can target lysosomes, which can be considered that the rapid synthesis of red emissive CDs from bacteria has good potential for bioimaging applications.

In conclusion, it is the first report on the synthesis of red emissive CDs by microwave–assisted hydrothermal method using PSB as a carbon source. The resultant CDs showed good water solubility and bright red fluorescence. More importantly, high concentrations of the red emissive CDs showed low cytotoxicity. Furthermore, the red emissive CDs present good lysosomal imaging effect. This work provides a facile approach for fabricating red emissive carbon–based nanomaterials applicable in bioimaging.

Jingmin Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Jing An: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Guanghui Zhang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Ruoyu Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yifan Xu: Validation, Formal analysis. Fenfang Zhu: Validation, Formal analysis. Kangliang Shen: Validation, Formal analysis. Yongzhong Wang: Conceptualization, Project administration, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financed by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Y Z Wang, grant No. 31770066 and 31470218) and the Open Fund for Anhui Key Laboratory of Modern Biomanufacturing of Anhui University (J M Wang.). All co–authors acknowledge the support of the Key Laboratory of Human Microenvironment and Precision Medicine of Anhui Higher Education Institutes, Anhui University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2021.131093.
